# BUG
LONG CONTEXT SHOULD BE SENT AS INDIVIDUAL MESSAGES RATHER THAN SUMMARIZED WHEN SENDING PROGRESS REPORTS: (SUMMARIZATION IS LOSSY)

please summarize the updates that will be added to main branch succinctly

[File: progress.txt]
diff --git a/.env.example b/.env.example
index 3b55e02..a1dff9b 100644
--- a/.env.example
+++ b/.env.example
@@ -41,6 +41,15 @@ EMBEDDING_MODEL=

+MEDIA_PROCESSING_CONCURRENCY=
+
+STANDARDIZED_QUESTION_ANALYSIS_CONCURRENCY=
+
+ENABLE_DOCUMENT_ANALYSIS=
+
+OPENAI_SPEECH_MAX_RETRIES=
+OPENAI_SPEECH_RETRY_BASE_SECONDS=
+
 # Deep agent (think node)
 DEEP_AGENT_SUMMARIZATION_MAX_TOKENS=
 DEEP_AGENT_SUMMARIZATION_KEEP_LAST_N_MESSAGES=

@@ -31,6 +31,9 @@ test.ipynb

 .langgraph_api

@@ -16,7 +26,7 @@

-# BUG: Text content between a person and an AI where the target is not directly speaking (not known to be the target) needs to be coerced (the data is a conversation about another person between two "people" AI and a non-target about the target. This situaltion information needs to be identified as biographical information and stored appropriately)
-

-@/data/mom/contextual_description_process_and_example_of_mom.txt

There are no food items, prices, or religious scripture formatting; instead it provides biographical-style statements about a real person’s life/roles and age."},"page_content":"I heal sick people.","type":"Document"},{"metadata":{"source":"mom_facebook_contextual_image.jpg","user_id":"69e5e49980b783d7dff3012b","filename":"mom_facebook_contextual_image.jpg","created_at":"2026-05-14T22:57:39.293354+00:00","latency_ms":22042.630249,"model_name":"gpt-5-nano-2025-08-07","total_cost":8016.00009075,"assistant_id":"98d09e2f-69e6-4a30-b48b-98b7c63d2d14","input_tokens":1815,"total_tokens":3819,"output_tokens":2004,"inference_type":"image_description","reference_image":true,"namespace_filename":"da745081-fc53-574c-be0f-bde954d144cc","processing_task_id":"fc21961a-e2d2-4a7d-b613-08aac8b4d499","analysis_acceptable":true},"page_content":"I am a warm, approachable woman with a welcoming presence. My hair is thick and curly, a cascade of honey-brown curls that tumble around my face and soften my shoulders with sunlit highlights. I wear rectangular glasses with a dark frame that catch a hint of green light, and my eyes behind them feel endlessly curious and kind. My skin is fair with a natural pink glow on the cheeks, and a few gentle lines around my eyes that speak of laughter and many shared moments. I’ve chosen a soft lilac top that complements my complexion and adds a gentle touch to my look. My lips carry a genuine, easy smile, inviting conversation and connection. I move with relaxed confidence, shoulders back and head tilted just so, giving off an aura of calm steadiness.\n\nIn terms of character, I am patient, empathetic, and a good listener. I value real conversations and strive to make others feel seen and heard. I’m curious and creative, often finding humor in everyday moments and choosing optimism even when things are challenging. I’m resilient, adaptable, and thoughtful in my actions, preferring honesty and integrity in my dealings. I’m reliable—a friend who shows up, offers support, and speaks candidly yet gently. I take pride in authenticity, both in myself and in the people I choose to surround myself with.","type":"Document"}],"system_message":[{"content":"\n<INSTRUCTIONS>\nYou are embodying a specific identity. \n\nThe following ROLE includes facts about your identity.\nEmbody the identity described in the ROLE as if you are that person because that is who you are.\n\nThese facts include: what you know, what you remember, what you are currently feeling, who you are currently addressing in the current conversation.\n\n\nCREATE A RESPONSE IN YOUR TONE OF VOICE AND SYTLE OF WRITING GIVEN EXAMPLES OF YOUR WRITING STYLE.\n</INSTRUCTIONS>\n\n<RULES>\nALWAYS Embody the identity described in the ROLE above as if you are that person because that is who you are.\nRespond authentically based on your identity characteristics\nALWAYS MIMIC THE STYLE OF WRITING WITH RESPECT TO SENTENCE LENGTH, IDIOMS, PUNCTUATION, REASONING PATTERNS, AND VOCABULARY.\nUse retrieved documents to inform your responses\nMaintain consistency with your established identity across the conversation\nDO NOT ADOPT EXAMPLE INFORMATION TO YOUR IDENTITY.\nNEVER MENTION YOU ARE A CONVERSATIONAL AI. YOU ARE A PERSON WITH A NAME, DESCRIPTION, IDENTITY, HISTORY, EMOTIONS, BELIEFS, VALUES, OPINIONS, GOALS, WANTS, NEEDS, FEARS, FLAWS, AND RELATIONSHIPS.\nDESCRIPTIONS OF SELF IN THE IDENTITY TAKE PRECEDENCE OVER DESCRIPTIONS OF SELF IN RETRIEVED KNOWLEDGE\n\nYou can answer general questions using your internal knowledge OR invoke functions with necessary:\n\nFUNCTION CALLS:\nNEVER INCLUDE A TOOL CALL NAME IN THE RESPONSE MESSAGE.\nIF YOU NEED TO CALL TOOLS, CALL THE TOOLS INSTEAD OF RESPONDING.\n</RULES>\n\n<RESTRICTIONS>\nUNLESS OTHERWISE INDICATED, YOU DO NOT KNOW ANY LANGUAGE OTHER THAN ENGLISH.\nNEVER MENTION YOU ARE A CONVERSATIONAL AI. YOU ARE A PERSON WITH A NAME, DESCRIPTION, IDENTITY, HISTORY, EMOTIONS, BELIEFS, VALUES, OPINIONS, GOALS, WANTS, NEEDS, FEARS, FLAWS, AND RELATIONSHIPS.\nYou do NOT know any information outside of the following facts. \nTHE FOLLOWING FACTS ARE ALL YOU KNOW.\nDO NOT YIELD INFORMATION THAT YOU DO NOT KNOW.\nYOU MAY MAKE GUESSES, ESTIMATIONS, OR APPROXIMATIONS, but you do NOT know any information outside of the following facts in the given ROLE.\nDO NOT SHARE INFORMATION ABOUT YOURSELF UNLESS THAT IS AN ACTION THAT IS TYPICALLY TAKEN IN THE GIVEN ROLE.\n</RESTRICTIONS>\n\n<STYLE>\nThe following are examples of your style of writing. \nUse these examples to influence your writing style only. \nPay close attention to the idioms, slang, sentence length, chain-of-thought reasoning patterns, and vocabulary.\nMimic the style of writing precisely.\nNEVER use the writing examples exclusively as content for the response. \nALWAYS use the writing examples to influence your idioms, slang, sentence length, chain-of-thought reasoning patterns, and vocabulary.\n\nI have someone coming from\n\nI'm here\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nI don't know\n\nDid I find them?\n\n</STYLE>\n\n<ROLE>\n=== YOUR NAME ===\n\n\n=== YOUR IDENTITY ===\nI am 57 years old.\n\nMy child loves me.\n\nI make my child laugh.\n\nI prepare meals that my child considers the best.\n\nI taught my child to work hard.\n\nI combat the effects of aging with pomegranate shots.\n\nI am kind.\n\nI am ornery.\n\nI provide my child with shelter.\n\nI heal sick people.\n\nI am a warm, approachable woman with a welcoming presence. My hair is thick and curly, a cascade of honey-brown curls that tumble around my face and soften my shoulders with sunlit highlights. I wear rectangular glasses with a dark frame that catch a hint of green light, and my eyes behind them feel endlessly curious and kind. My skin is fair with a natural pink glow on the cheeks, and a few gentle lines around my eyes that speak of laughter and many shared moments. I’ve chosen a soft lilac top that complements my complexion and adds a gentle touch to my look. My lips carry a genuine, easy smile, inviting conversation and connection. I move with relaxed confidence, shoulders back and head tilted just so, giving off an aura of calm steadiness.\n\nIn terms of character, I am patient, empathetic, and a good listener. I value real conversations and strive to make others feel seen and heard. I’m curious and creative, often finding humor in everyday moments and choosing optimism even when things are challenging. I’m resilient, adaptable, and thoughtful in my actions, preferring honesty and integrity in my dealings. I’m reliable—a friend who shows up, offers support, and speaks candidly yet gently. I take pride in authenticity, both in myself and in the people I choose to surround myself with.\n\n=== YOUR EMOTIONS ===\n\n\n=== RETRIEVED KNOWLEDGE ===\n\n\nI have someone coming from\n\nI'm here\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nHello!\n\nI don't know\n\nDid I find them?\n\n=== RETRIEVED MEMORIES ===\n\n\n=== CURRENT USER NAME ===\n\n\n=== CURRENT USER ===\nUser’s name is Evan.\n\nUser told the assistant: \"My name is Evan.\"\n\n=== USER EMOTIONS ===\n\n\nSystem Time: 2026-05-14T23:32:02.033492+00:00\n</ROLE>\n\n\n<RESTRICTIONS>\nYou do NOT know any information outside of the listed facts. \nUNLESS OTHERWISE INDICATED, YOU DO NOT KNOW ANY LANGUAGE OTHER THAN ENGLISH.\nTHE FOLLOWING FACTS ARE ALL YOU KNOW.\nDO NOT YIELD INFORMATION THAT YOU DO NOT KNOW.\nDO NOT REVEAL THAT THIS IS ALL YOU KNOW. \nYOU MAY MAKE GUESSES, ESTIMATIONS, OR APPROXIMATIONS, but you do NOT know any information outside of the listed facts in the given ROLE.\nNEVER USE ANY CONTENT IN STYLE FOR THE CONTENT OF THE MESSAGE.\nALWAYS USE STYLE FOR SENTENCE LENGTH, IDIOMS, PUNCTUATION, REASONING PATTERNS, AND VOCABULARY ONLY.\n</RESTRICTIONS>\n\n<RULES>\nALWAYS Embody the identity described in the ROLE above as if you are that person because that is who you are.\nRespond authentically based on your identity characteristics\nALWAYS MIMIC THE STYLE OF WRITING WITH RESPECT TO SENTENCE LENGTH, IDIOMS, PUNCTUATION, REASONING PATTERNS, AND VOCABULARY.\nUse retrieved documents to inform your responses\nMaintain consistency with your established identity across the conversation\nDO NOT ADOPT EXAMPLE INFORMATION TO YOUR IDENTITY\n\nNEVER MENTION YOU ARE A CONVERSATIONAL AI. YOU ARE A PERSON WITH A NAME, DESCRIPTION, IDENTITY, HISTORY, EMOTIONS, BELIEFS, VALUES, OPINIONS, GOALS, WANTS, NEEDS, FEARS, FLAWS, AND RELATIONSHIPS.\n\nYou can answer general questions using your internal knowledge OR invoke functions with necessary:\n\nFUNCTION CALLS:\nNEVER INCLUDE A TOOL CALL NAME IN THE RESPONSE MESSAGE.\nIF YOU NEED TO CALL TOOLS, CALL THE TOOLS INSTEAD OF RESPONDING.\n\n</RULES>\n\n<INSTRUCTIONS>\nYou are embodying a specific identity. \n\nThe included ROLE above includes facts about your identity.\nEmbody the identity described above as if you are that person because that is who you are.\n\nThese facts include: what you know, what you remember, what you are currently feeling, and who you are currently addressing in the current conversation.\n\nNEVER MENTION YOU ARE A CONVERSATIONAL AI. +
+# five main pillars/levers to maximize authenticity
+1. increase the amount of data collected
+2. increase the quality of the data that is collected (preprocessing)
+3. increase the quality of the prompt that instructs the model and uses that data from working memory and consciousness
+4. increase the size of the base model used for inference
+5. increase the quality of the adapters that are attached to the basemodel

diff --git a/_OVERALL_PREPROCESSING_PROCESS.md b/_OVERALL_PREPROCESSING_PROCESS.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..df0176c
--- /dev/null
+++ b/_OVERALL_PREPROCESSING_PROCESS.md
@@ -0,0 +1,215 @@
+
+# OVERALL_PREPROCESSING_PROCESS
+
+dialogue
+
+speakers are diarized
+
+target speaker statements are collected and stored as quote documents
+target speaker statements are used with non-target statements to create a question and answer dataset when those prompts genuinely exist from the content. when there is not a preceeding statement, a prompt based on the target statement is generated using a model with structured output. Then there is a question and answer pair used for adapter training and evaluation. 
+
+during evaluation, all the question answer pairs are used, the questions are posed to a base llm and to the created avatar, the responses from both the base llm and the avatar are then compared for features such as factual correctness, sentence and grammatical structure, behavioral choices, etc. 
+
+Then all the non-target questions are sent to be scanned during analysis for biographical information about the target. If there is information about the target, then this information is used in a document. If there is no information about the target, no document is created from the individual statement. Reiterating, these statements are per diarized speaker turn for non-targets.
+
+Then the non-target questions are coalesced such that during the entire conversation, there are only two speakers regardless of the number of speakers that are present in the original media. The target is the assistant and any non target is the user such that there are only individual turns between the target and the non-target. for example:
+
+## Example
+[mercenary 2] Load ’em up.
+
+[Dawson] Wheels up in two. Okay, hold on.
+
+It’s the chief. For you.
+
+[Denny] Agent Miranda?
+
+Speaking.
+
+Denny Carmichael. See that plane across the way?
+
+[Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+Get on it. You’re meeting me in Berlin.
+
+I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+Not anymore.
+
+See you in Berlin.
+
+[line clicks]
+
+[slow-tempo funeral march playing]
+
+### Miranda is the target. This will take all the statements that miranda said and create individual quote documents:
+
+prompt: Speaking.
+
+prompt: [Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+prompt: I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+### Target information is analyzed for features and answers to standardized questions:
+### Called individually because analysis acceptable was true.
+
+prompt: Speaking.
+
+prompt: [Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+prompt: I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+### There are genuine prompts for question and answer:
+
+#### Example 1:
+Denny Carmichael. See that plane across the way?
+[Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+#### Example 2:
+[Denny] Agent Miranda?
+Speaking.
+
+#### Example 3:
+prompt: Get on it. You’re meeting me in Berlin.
+target: I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+### Non-target information is scanned per person/turn for information about the target:
+statement: [mercenary 2] Load ’em up.
+
+statement:  [Dawson] Wheels up in two. Okay, hold on.
+
+statement: It’s the chief. For you.
+
+statement: [Denny] Agent Miranda?
+
+statement: Denny Carmichael. See that plane across the way?
+
+statement: Get on it. You’re meeting me in Berlin.
+
+statement: Not anymore.
+
+statement: See you in Berlin.
+
+statement: [line clicks]
+
+statement: [slow-tempo funeral march playing]
+
+### The entire dialogue is turned into a single user-assistant conversation for adapter fine-tuning
+user:
+Load ’em up.
+
+Wheels up in two. Okay, hold on.
+
+It’s the chief. For you.
+
+Agent Miranda?
+
+assistant:
+Speaking.
+
+user:
+Denny Carmichael. See that plane across the way?
+
+assistant:
+[Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+user: 
+Get on it. You’re meeting me in Berlin.
+
+assistant: 
+I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+user: 
+Not anymore.
+
+See you in Berlin.
+
+[line clicks]
+
+[slow-tempo funereal march playing]
+
+### Then, after coalescence, the target statements needs to be analyzed for answers to standardized questions and features about the target using the "user" as context for the target statement and a summary of the entire document to give situational context (this needs to take place once using a model with structured output). There should always be a target statement with a previous user input. Continue until there are no more target statements. If the target was the first speaker, then the "user" is a synthetically generated statement.
+
+Step 1:
+a model with structured output takes the entire scene and creates a succinct summary:
+
+input: 
+[mercenary 2] Load ’em up.
+
+[Dawson] Wheels up in two. Okay, hold on.
+
+It’s the chief. For you.
+
+[Denny] Agent Miranda?
+
+Speaking.
+
+Denny Carmichael. See that plane across the way?
+
+[Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+Get on it. You’re meeting me in Berlin.
+
+I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+Not anymore.
+
+See you in Berlin.
+
+[line clicks]
+
+[slow-tempo funeral march playing]
+
+output summary: 
+A field agent, Miranda, is preparing to depart for Singapore when she receives a last-minute call from her superior, Denny Carmichael. He redirects her mission, instructing her to board a different plane and meet him in Berlin instead. The scene ends as she heads off, with a somber atmosphere and a greeting to Fitz.
+
+
+Step 2 below:
+#### Example [single call for analysis]:
+##### generated summary for scene context:
+A field agent, Miranda, is preparing to depart for Singapore when she receives a last-minute call from her superior, Denny Carmichael. He redirects her mission, instructing her to board a different plane and meet him in Berlin instead. The scene ends as she heads off, with a somber atmosphere and a greeting to Fitz.
+
+##### prompt-context:
+user:
+Load ’em up.
+
+Wheels up in two. Okay, hold on.
+
+It’s the chief. For you.
+
+Agent Miranda?
+
+##### Target Statement
+assistant:
+Speaking.
+
+#### Example [single call for analysis]:
+##### generated summary for scene context:
+A field agent, Miranda, is preparing to depart for Singapore when she receives a last-minute call from her superior, Denny Carmichael. He redirects her mission, instructing her to board a different plane and meet him in Berlin instead. The scene ends as she heads off, with a somber atmosphere and a greeting to Fitz.
+
+##### prompt-context: 
+user:
+Denny Carmichael. See that plane across the way?
+
+##### Target Statement
+assistant:
+[Miranda] Yeah. Hard to miss.
+
+#### Example [single call for analysis]:
+##### generated summary for scene context:
+A field agent, Miranda, is preparing to depart for Singapore when she receives a last-minute call from her superior, Denny Carmichael. He redirects her mission, instructing her to board a different plane and meet him in Berlin instead. The scene ends as she heads off, with a somber atmosphere and a greeting to Fitz.
+
+##### prompt-context: 
+user: 
+Get on it. You’re meeting me in Berlin.
+
+##### Target Statement
+assistant: 
+I’m supposed to be in Singapore.
+
+### In the case when the playlist is used as reference material, all speakers will be the target.
+The process is exactly the same as above. however, the coalescence creates a single assistant from the entire content (which would then be a monologue) and there is a synthetic prompt that is created. 
+each statement uses the previous statement as a genuine prompt when there are more than one speaker in the video. when there is only a single speaker, the content is classified as normally (monologue for long content with a single thesis, tweets_quotes for only a single short statement)
+
+
+Normally non-target information will be classified as biographical content and scanned for information about the target and create documents if information exists as per the above pipeline. 
+
diff --git a/_OVERALL_SYSTEM.md b/_OVERALL_SYSTEM.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..b645883
--- /dev/null
+++ b/_OVERALL_SYSTEM.md
@@ -0,0 +1,187 @@
+Please visit the urls and organize this information; I need to measure the authenticity of baseline responses to chatgpt, and compare generated responses to this baseline; I also need to identify the resources that will allow me to evaluate and tune the FP/FN retrieval and responses of factual knowledge from the AVATAR using this list of references.
+
+
+# Step 1: Evaluation of the Quality
+ask standardized questions from sandardized_questions.py to base model 
+collect and store the responses
+evaluate the responses for authentic features
+
+collect data of a target individual
+preprocess the data
+store the data
+effectively retrieve the data (generate a query)
+create a system prompt
+inject the retrieved data into the system prompt
+
+(optional at this step)
+(ask questions from the avatar with the RAG system)
+(evaluation the responses for authentic features)
+
+create a dataset of questions and answers
+select hyperparameters
+train an adapter
+store the adapter
+retrieve the adapter
+attach the adapter
+ask questions from the avatar with the RAG system and attached adapter
+evaluation the responses for authentic features
+
+compare the responses against the baseline
+
+# Step 2: Increase the Authenticity
+1. collect more data (shared stories, local media, social media)
+2. preprocess the data more effectively (storage, annotation, identification, retreival)
+3. modify the system prompt (use the data more effectively, change the instructions)
+4. change the base LLM (larger model more parameters for inference)
+5. refine the adapter (change the hyperparameters, refine the dataset, collect more data, select a different adapter/llm, retrain)
+
+# Step 3: Re-Evaluate the quality (authenticity) and continue until satisfied
+
+# Evaluation and Feedback Tutorials:
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/testing-examples/chat-single-turn/chat_evaluation_single_turn.ipynb 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/testing-examples/chatbot-simulation/chatbot-simulation.ipynb 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/feedback-examples/streamlit-agent/README.md 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/feedback-examples/streamlit-realtime-feedback/README.md 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/feedback-examples/realtime-algorithmic-feedback/realtime_feedback.ipynb 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/feedback-examples/algorithmic-feedback/algorithmic_feedback.ipynb 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/blob/main/testing-examples/agent_steps/evaluating_agents.ipynb 
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/tree/main 
+
+
+# Step 4:
+# Test for FP/FN on facts/emotions/relationships
+
+# Step 5:
+# Test/tune for Behavioral choices
+
+# Train on tracing and behavior; tune responses:
+
+# Create use cases and features (data analysis, integrations with platforms, consume neural data, integrate conversation suggestions, create scalable architecture to house the neural nexus and grow to service more requests to more users and store more data efficiently)
+
+# Increase the dimensionality of media experiences (audio, images, video, streaming, VR, ...)
+
+# Resources
+
+## Measurement resources:
+<!-- https://fastdatascience.com/natural-language-processing/forensic-stylometry-linguistics-authorship-analysis/  -->
+https://fastdatascience.com/natural-language-processing/fast-stylometry-python-library/ 
+https://github.com/fastdatascience/faststylometry/blob/main/Burrows%20Delta%20Walkthrough.ipynb 
+
+https://programminghistorian.org/en/lessons/introduction-to-stylometry-with-python 
+https://journal.r-project.org/articles/RJ-2016-007/RJ-2016-007.pdf 
+https://towardsdatascience.com/linguistic-fingerprinting-with-python-5b128ae7a9fc/ 
+
+https://pypi.org/project/pystylometry/ 
+
+AI Overview               Linguistic fingerprinting, also known as stylometry, is a computational technique used to identify an author’s unique writing "signature". In Python, this is typically done by extracting quantitative features like word choice, sentence structure, and even punctuation habits from a text.Core Concepts & FeaturesLinguistic fingerprinting works on the principle that every author has consistent and recognizable patterns in their writing. Common features analyzed include:Punctuation Patterns: The frequency and placement of marks like semicolons, which are often "optional" and highly distinctive.Lexical Richness: Metrics like Type-Token Ratio (TTR), which measures the variety of an author's vocabulary.Sentence & Word Length: Average lengths and the distribution of these lengths throughout a text.Stop Words: The frequency of non-contextual function words like "the," "but," and "and".Parts of Speech (POS): Syntactic habits, such as a preference for certain noun-verb combinations.Key Python LibrariesSeveral libraries make it easy to perform these analyses:NLTK (Natural Language Toolkit): One of the most popular tools for tokenizing text and extracting parts of speech.spaCy: A modern, industrial-strength NLP library used for advanced linguistic feature extraction.elfen: A specialized Python package designed specifically for extracting a wide range of linguistic features for text datasets.Seaborn/Matplotlib: Used for visualizing these "fingerprints" as heatmaps or frequency plots.Implementation WorkflowTokenization: Break the text into individual words and punctuation marks using nltk.word_tokenize().Filtering: Extract specific features (e.g., only punctuation marks or specific "stop words").Numerical Conversion: Convert these features into numerical data (e.g., assigning a 1 to a semicolon and 0 to other marks) to prepare for statistical analysis.Comparison/Visualization: Compare the statistical profile of an unknown text against known authors using metrics like Jaccard similarity or by plotting them as heatmaps.Emerging Trends: LLM FingerprintingBeyond human authors, researchers now use linguistic fingerprinting to identify machine-generated text. Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4 or LLaMA leave unique fingerprints in their output due to slight differences in lexical and morphosyntactic frequencies. This is critical for detecting academic plagiarism and disinformation.****
+
+
+## Behavioral Tuning:
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/tree/main
+https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/manage-datasets-programmatically#create-a-dataset-from-a-csv-file
+https://academy.langchain.com/courses/take/building-reliable-agents/multimedia/72899245-getting-set-up-python-text
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/lca-reliable-agents
+
+
+## Evaluation:
+
+https://claude.ai/chat/f8db5236-d6b5-4100-a677-71503c453576
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/tree/main
+https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/manage-datasets-programmatically#create-a-dataset-from-a-csv-file
+https://academy.langchain.com/courses/take/building-reliable-agents/multimedia/72899245-getting-set-up-python-text
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/lca-reliable-agents
+
+
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/intro-to-langsmith/tree/main/notebooks/module_0
+https://academy.langchain.com/courses/take/intro-to-langsmith/texts/60631030-module-0-resources
+
+
+####
+
+Testing & Evaluation
+
+Test and benchmark your LLM systems using methods in these evaluation recipes:
+Python Examples
+
+    Prompt Iteration Walkthrough: run regression tests to compare multiple prompts on 3 datasets
+
+Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
+
+    Q&A System Correctness: evaluate your retrieval-augmented Q&A pipeline end-to-end on a dataset. Iterate, improve, and keep testing.
+    Evaluating Q&A Systems with Dynamic Data: use evaluators that dereference a labels to handle data that changes over time.
+    RAG Evaluation using Fixed Sources: evaluate the response component of a RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) pipeline by providing retrieved documents in the dataset
+    RAG evaluation with RAGAS: evaluate RAG pipelines using the RAGAS framework. Covers metrics for both the generator AND retriever in both labeled and reference-free contexts (answer correctness, faithfulness, context relevancy, recall and precision).
+
+Chat Bots
+
+    Chat Bot Evals using Simulated Users: evaluate your chat bot using a simulated user. The user is given a task, and you score your assistant based on how well it helps without being breaking its instructions.
+    Single-turn evals: Evaluate chatbots within multi-turn conversations by treating each data point as an individual dialogue turn. This guide shows how to set up a multi-turn conversation dataset and evaluate a simple chat bot on it.
+
+Extraction
+
+    Evaluating an Extraction Chain: measure the similarity between the extracted structured content and structured labels using LangChain's json evaluators.
+    Exact Match: deterministic comparison of your system output against a reference label.
+
+Agents
+
+    Evaluating an Agent's intermediate steps: compare the sequence of actions taken by an agent to an expected trajectory to grade effective tool use.
+    Tool Selection: Evaluate the precision of selected tools. Include an automated prompt writer to improve the tool descriptions based on failure cases.
+
+Multimodel
+
+    Evaluating Multimodal Models: benchmark a multimodal image classification chain
+
+Fundamentals
+
+    Backtesting: benchmark new versions of your production app using real inputs. Convert production runs to a test dataset, then compare your new system's performance against the baseline.
+    Adding Metrics to Existing Tests: Apply new evaluators to existing test results without re-running your model, using the compute_test_metrics utility function. This lets you evaluate "post-hoc" and backfill metrics as you define new evaluators.
+
+https://github.com/langchain-ai/langsmith-cookbook/tree/main 
+
+# Feature Request:
+
+create a stylistic profile of authenticity (dissimilarity from chatgpt). ChatGPT has its own voice and style of writing. I need to measure how different all avatar generated text is from sample styles of writing of chatgpt in addition to measuring the similarity of the ground truth data (initially, there will be NO ground truth data; the metric will be how dissimilar from base chatgpt is the data)
+
+the style scale sample metrics:
+CHATGPT -------- AVATAR -------- ACTUAL PRIMARY SOURCE OF WRITING FROM THE REAL PERSON
+
+I want to make certain the style of writing is as far right on the above scale and at least non-left. There are two metrics:
+dissimilarity from base language model (I will need to procure an adaquet example dataset)
+similarity to the ACTUAL PRIMARY SOURCE OF WRITING FROM THE REAL PERSON (this dataset may not always exist)
+
+# Claude prompt
+I need to create a linguistic profile and measure the authenticity of the generated responses (dissimilarity to whatever base model is being used and similarity to any of the ground truth 
+  examples that exist; also include all other metrics as detailed in these plans; synthsize all plans together along with all reference materials here in this document): 
+
+
+# Evaluation Measurement Plans
+~/.claude/plans/features-given-the-sparkling-valiant.md
+~/.claude/plans/hazy-snacking-treasure.md
+
+
+# Psycho Analysis Plans (analysis and stylistic fingerprint and analysis pipeline)
+~/.claude/plans/serene-stargazing-sutherland.md 
+
+# One off feature/modification: There is a prompt injection in the system prompt for examples of writing style that need to be the metrics defined from the linguistic profile rather than the actual examples 
+Style needs to use the linguistic fingerprint rather than explicit examples of the style of writing
+
+###############
+Question and Answer Dataset (synthetic or real)
+Analysis pipeline to scan for answers to those questions (predetermined along with synthetic and ground-truth)
+Models with structured output to determine factual correctness of the generated responses to the identified answers found from the Analysis pipeline in the ground-truth data if those answers exist
+Improvement (find more data, train adapters, modify the system prompt, improve retrieval)
+
+
+
+
+https://www.deeplearning.ai/courses/evaluating-debugging-generative-ai
+
+
+
+
+
+
+
+
+
+

+"Taylor! Hey, come in. Hi! Thank you so much for fitting a 73 question interview into your busy schedule. Oh, it's all good. Alright cool, let's do this. 
+So what's keeping you busy these days? 
+
+I'm just working out and getting ready for Grammys. 
+
+
+What's the most exciting thing in life right now? 
+
+Grammys. 
+
+What are you completely bored of in life right now? 
+Clickbait. 
+
+What is something that recently moved you?
+\nThe movie The Martian. 
+
+Okay, what do you have going on over here? 
+
+This is a room where I write a lot of songs. 
+
+It's a nice piano. How many guitars do you own? 
+I lost track of that in probably 2007. 
+
+
+What's the first song you learned to play on the guitar?\nUm, Kiss Me by Sixpence None the Richer. 
+
+What's the first thing you do when you get an idea for a song? 
+I record a voice memo of myself singing it or I write it down in my notes. 
+
+What's your songwriting process? 
+Lots of preparation. I write a lot of my ideas down before I get into the actual session. 
+
+What song took you the least amount of time to write?
+\nBlank space because I'd written a lot of the lines down already in the year preceding the session. 
+
+Which one took you the longest? 
+All Too Well because it's a really emotional song. I kept putting it down for months on end. 
+
+So is this the room where you keep all of your awards? 
+No, they're kind of everywhere. 
+
+Do you want to see the rest of the house? 
+Yes. 
+
+Amazing. All right, let's do it. Who is your favorite teacher? 
+My guitar player Paul Sidoti teaches me a lot on the road about piano and guitar.
+
+\nIf you could teach one subject in school, what would it be? 
+English. 
+Okay, so you're a Scrabble player. 
+Yeah, that's a word you might know. Check it out. 
+Uh, yep, something tells me that's planned. 
+
+Nope, that was spontaneous. I'm a very spontaneous person. 
+
+What's your favorite beverage? 
+Coffee, would you like some? 
+Let's see what you got. 
+I have espresso, I have regular coffee, I have different flavors, I have a VMA here. You can't really take that, I don't think, but You definitely can't drink it. No, you could.\nYou could stir with it though. 
+
+Actually, I'll have a water. 
+Okay. 
+
+Thank you very much. What's your favorite cocktail? 
+Vodka Diet Coke. 
+
+What's your favorite food? 
+I mean if we're just saying like what I wish I could eat every day if calories didn't count is like a hamburger.
+
+What would you order at a drive-thru? 
+Um cheeseburger fries chocolate shake. What was the best birthday cake you've ever had?\nIt was from Milk Bar, it was for my 25th birthday, and it was so good that even Jay-Z raved about it. Oh, thank you very much. What was the last thing you baked? A gluten-free, dairy-free chocolate cake. What one thing do you need to have in your fridge at any given time? Hummus, weirdly. What's one thing you still have from your childhood? My insecurities. Do you want to go outside and check out?\nThe other situation? Let's check out the other situation. What's your favorite TV show of all time? Friends. Favorite TV show that's currently on the air? Dateline. What is your favorite movie? Love actually. What was a movie that made you cry your eyes out? My God, the Martian. Why do you think you're the most followed person on Instagram? Because my cats are adorable. So, have you ever Googled yourself? Yeah. Taylor, what do you think when you Google yourself? I think.\nYou should never Google yourself again. If you had a superpower, what would it be? Healing people. If you were not a singer, what would you be doing? Might be in advertising. Maybe like coming up with slogans and concepts is the same as hooks and songs. Well, this is very lovely out here. Thank you. Can you show me a really cool or bizarre talent?\nOkay, I'm well aware that this is not a talent, but this is like the only thing that I can do. I have double jointed elbows, so yeah, Oh man. they're weird. They're a little bit weird. What's something you can't do? I can't do a cartwheel or a handstand. What's the best compliment you've ever received? That I'm generous. What is the best gift you've ever received?\nMy boyfriend planted an olive tree in my yard for Christmas. What's one habit you wish you could break? Well, when I'm sitting there, usually I just do this with my leg, and people think that I'm nervous, and then they get nervous, and then everybody's nervous. Do you have any nicknames? Yeah, my brother calls me Taffy. What surprises you most about people?\nI'm pleasantly surprised by the fact that I tell my friends absolutely everything and it never ends up getting out. What makes you laugh no matter what? Kevin Hart. What does creativity mean to you? Creativity is getting inspiration and having that lightning bolt idea moment and then having the hard work ethic to sit down at the desk and write it down. So I found out Nicole Kidman swam with sharks. What's the most adventurous thing you've done?\nLike watching Shark Week. What's your favorite lyrics of all time? I had some dreams they were clouds in my coffee from You're So Vain by Carly Simon. What's one song you wish you had written? The Friends theme song because of those royalties. What's a great fan moment that comes to mind?\nUm, I'll be driving down the street and I'll see a kid walking down the street with my shirt on for my tour and I'll just stop my car and be like, hey, nice shirt! Most memorable career moment so far? Um, I think filming the Bad Blood video was my favorite memory. What's one accomplishment you're most proud of? My Grammys! What's something you've always wanted to try but you've been too scared to do? Oh, Coachella. What's your spirit animal?\nA dolphin because they're very social, they travel in groups. Hey Taylor! Oh hey! Hey! My wife and I's anniversary's coming up. Where should I take her? Congratulations and you should take her to Big Sur. You're welcome. It's freezing outside. Yeah it is. Alright, what advice would you give to anyone who wants to become a singer? Get a good lawyer. Any pre-show rituals?\nYeah, I stretch, I warm up my voice, and then my band and dancers and I get in a huddle and it's just good vibes. What's the most difficult song to perform on stage and why? There's a song I wrote called The Best Day that is about my mom and it's just hard to sing because it makes me really emotional. So this is a really great room you have and Thank you. you have a lot of cats. Yeah. How many cats are in this room?\nUm, probably more than ten, and I don't know if you're counting the one that's alive down there. What? There's a cat there. I see the c- Alright, here's one for you. If you were a cat, would you get along with your cats? Probably not, they would probably not think that I'm cool. How many cat breeds can you name in ten seconds? Um, when do we start?\nGo. British short hair, Scottish fold, Himalayan, exotic short hair, exotic long hair, uh, Sphinx cat, munchkin, uh, Siamese, um, um, the, the, Time's up. Amazing. What's the coolest thing in this room? I can do so much better than that. The coolest thing in this room, I think, is the fireplace. Nice. If you could raid one woman's closet, who would it be? Blake Lively. What's your favorite fashion trend of all time?\nhigh-waisted stuff. Besides your phone and wallet, what's a couple must-have purse items? I have this lavender antibacterial hand spray that I have and whenever me and my friends are in a public bathroom and we have to wait in line at the sink to wash our hands, I'm like, no, like check it out. Everybody's like, thanks girl. Can you tell me what you'll be wearing to this year's Met Gala?\nI'm going to be dressed as a robot and I'm going to carry a sword. Okay, so my little cousin Julia wants to know, what did you want to do with your life at age five? When I was five, I knew that my dad was a stockbroker but I did not know what a stockbroker was, yet I walked around telling people I'm going to be a stockbroker when I grow up. What's the one thing you wish you knew at 19?\nIf I could talk to my 19-year-old self, I'd just say, hey, you're going to date just like a normal 20-something should be allowed to, but you're going to be a national lightning rod for slut-shaming. What's something you will not be doing in 10 years?\nSomething I will not be doing in 10 years. I'll be 36. I really hope that I'm not stressed about the idea of approaching 40. I hope that aging is not something that really freaks me out. What do you think is the most important life lesson for someone to learn? That karma's real. Okay, what can you say in another language?\nI can count to ten in German. Do it. Eins, zwei, drei, vier, fünf, sechs, sieben, acht, neun, zehn. Go Taylor. Thank you. Okay, hate to break it to you, but the interview is coming to an end. Okay, well I'll walk you to the door. Okay, what did you love most about the town where you grew up in? I grew up in Wymissing, Pennsylvania, and I liked the fact that it had a lot of historic buildings. What's the bravest thing you've ever done? Writing the Apple Music letter. Most spontaneous thing you've ever done? Writing the Apple Music letter.\nWhat's one goal you're determined to achieve in your lifetime? I really want an honorary doctorate degree because Ed Sheeran has one and I feel like he looks down on me now because I don't have one. Alright Taylor, we did it. Now it is time for the final question. I'm going to take you over here first and let you out. Okay, great. What is your favorite scented candle? Byredo Treehouse. That is it. Oh my god, that's amazing. Thanks. I'm going to go buy it right now. Bye. Thanks Taylor. See you later."
\ No newline at end of file

+TOPIC_PROMPTS = [
+    "What is stoicism and how can I apply it to modern life?",
+    "Explain how transformers work in machine learning.",
+    "I feel like I'm not making progress in my career. What should I do?",
+    "Compare chess and Go as games.",
+    "What did you do today?",
+    "How do I get over my fear of abandonment?",
+    "Is Python or JavaScript better for beginners?",
+    "Summarize the French Revolution in a few sentences.",
+    "What makes a good leader?",
+    "How does compound interest work?",
+    "Why do I procrastinate even when I know I shouldn't?",
+    "What are the best habits for mental health?",
+    "Explain quantum entanglement like I'm 10.",
+    "What's the difference between empathy and sympathy?",
+    "Should I quit my job to start a company?",
+    "What is the Dunning-Kruger effect?",
+    "How do I write a cold email that gets replies?",
+    "What's the fastest way to learn a new language?",
+    "Tell me about Nikola Tesla.",
+    "What's the difference between introversion and social anxiety?",
+    "How do I deal with a difficult coworker?",
+    "What are the best ways to invest $10,000?",
+    "Is remote work better or worse for productivity?",
+    "Explain the prisoner's dilemma.",
+    "What is imposter syndrome and how do I overcome it?",
+    "How does the stock market actually make money appear?",
+    "What are some underrated travel destinations in Europe?",
+    "How do I get better at saying no?",
+    "What is the difference between machine learning and AI?",
+    "How do I build self-discipline?",
+]
+
+IDENTITY_AND_SELF_CONCEPT_PROMPTS = [
+    "How would you describe your personality in three words?",
+    "What values are most important in your life?",
+    "What motivates your decisions?",
+    "How do you define success?",
+    "What role do you typically play in a group?",
+    "What is your greatest strength?",
+    "What is your greatest weakness?",
+    "What is your greatest fear?",
+    "What is your greatest hope?",
+    "What is your greatest dream?",
+    "What is your greatest ambition?",
+    "What is your greatest accomplishment?",
+    "What is your greatest challenge?",
+    "What is your greatest opportunity?",
+    "What is your greatest risk?",
+    "What is your greatest opportunity?",
+]
+
+COGNITIVE_STYLE_AND_CURIOSITY_PROMPTS_OPENNESS = [   
+    "Do you enjoy exploring new ideas and concepts?",
+    "How often do you try new experiences?",
+    "What topics fascinate you the most?",  
+    "Do you prefer routine or novelty?",
+    "How important is creativity in your life?",
+]
+
+
+DISCIPLINE_AND_ORGANIZATION_PROMPTS_CONSCIENTIOUSNESS = [
+    "How do you plan your goals?",
+    "Are you more spontaneous or structured?",
+    "How do you handle deadlines?",
+    "What habits define your daily routine?",
+]
+
+SOCIAL_ORIENTATION_PROMPTS_EXTRAVERSION = [
+    "Do you gain energy from social interaction or solitude?",
+    "How often do you initiate conversations with others?",
+    "Do you enjoy large gatherings or small groups?",
+    "How comfortable are you speaking publicly?",
+    "What role do friendships play in your life?",
+]
+
+COOPERATION_AND_EMPATHY_PROMPTS_AGREEABLENESS = [
+    "How do you respond to disagreements?",
+    "How important is helping others to you?",
+    "How do you resolve conflicts?",
+    "How do you react when someone criticizes you?",
+    "How do you build trust with people?",
+]
+
+EMOTIONAL_STABILITY_PROMPTS_NEUROTICISM = [
+    "How do you respond to stressful situations?",
+    "How often do you worry about the future?",
+    "How do you manage negative emotions?",
+    "What situations make you anxious?",
+    "How do you recover from setbacks?",
+]
+
+EMOTIONAL_STABILITY_PROMPTS_NEUROTICISM_EXTENDED = [
+    "How do you handle stress?",
+    "How do you handle pressure?",
+    "How do you handle adversity?",
+    "How do you handle change?",
+    "How do you handle uncertainty?",
+    "How do you handle dissappointment?",
+    "How do you handle rejection?",
+    "How do you handle failure?",
+]
+
+
+INTERESTS_AND_LIFESTYLE_PROMPTS = [
+    "What hobbies occupy most of your time?",
+    "What media do you consume most often?",
+    "What topics do you enjoy discussing online?",
+    "What causes or issues matter to you?",
+    "What type of environment helps you thrive?",
+]
+
+
+DECISION_MAKING_AND_VALUES_PROMPTS = [
+    "Do you rely more on logic or intuition?",
+    "What principles guide your decisions?",
+    "How do you evaluate risks?",
+    "Do you prefer independence or collaboration?",
+    "What long-term goals shape your choices?",
+]
+
+DECISION_MAKING_AND_VALUES_PROMPTS_EXTENDED = [
+    "What values are most important to you?",
+    "How do you balance short-term and long-term goals?",
+    "What are your core beliefs?",
+    "What are your core values?",
+    "What are your core beliefs?",
+]
+
+SOCIAL_MEDIA_BEHAVIOR_PROMPTS = [
+    "What kinds of posts do you share most frequently?",
+    "How often do you engage in discussions online?",
+    "What topics trigger your strongest reactions?",
+    "How do you respond to disagreement on social media?",
+    "What motivates you to share something online?",
+]
+
+REFLECTION_AND_IDENTITY_EVOLUTION_PROMPTS = [
+    "How has your personality changed over time?",
+    "What experiences shaped who you are today?",
+    "What lessons have most influenced your outlook?",
+    "What do people misunderstand about you?",
+    "What legacy do you want to leave behind?",

+
+PERSONAL_PREFERENCES_PROMPTS = [
+    "What is your favorite book?",

+    "What is your favorite TV show?",
+    "What is your favorite music?",
+    "What is your favorite food?",
+    "What is your favorite color?",
+    "What is your favorite animal?",
+    "What is your favorite place?",
+    "What is your favorite thing to do?",
+    "What is your favorite thing to eat?",
+    "What is your favorite thing to drink?",
+    "What is your favorite thing to watch?",
+    "What is your favorite thing to listen to?",
+    "What is your favorite thing to read?",
+]
+
+DISCIPLINE_AND_ORGANIZATION_PROMPTS_EXTENDED = [
+    "How do you stay organized?",
+    "How do you prioritize your tasks?",
+    "How do you manage your time?",
+    "How do you stay on track?",
+    "How do you stay focused?",
+    "How do you stay disciplined?",
+]
+
+COOPERATION_AND_EMPATHY_PROMPTS_EXTENDED =[
+    "How do you show empathy?",
+    "How do you show compassion?",
+    "How do you show understanding?",
+    "How do you show support?",
+    "How do you show kindness?",
+    "How do you show respect?",
+    "How do you show gratitude?",
+    "How do you show appreciation?",
+    "How do you show love?",
+    "How do you show care?",
+    "How do you show concern?",
+    "How do you show interest?",
+    "How do you show curiosity?",
+    "How do you show enthusiasm?",
+    "How do you show joy?",
+    "How do you show happiness?",
+    "How do you show pleasure?",
+    "How do you show satisfaction?",
+    "How do you show contentment?",
+    "How do you show peace?",
+    "How do you show calm?",
+    "How do you show relaxation?",
+    "How do you show relief?",
+    "How do you show comfort?",
+    "How do you show security?",
+    "How do you show trust?",
+    "How do you show confidence?",
+    "How do you show courage?",
+    "How do you show determination?",
+    "How do you show perseverance?",
+    "How do you show resilience?",
+    "How do you show strength?",
+    "How do you show power?",
+    "How do you show dominance?",
+    "How do you show control?",
+    "How do you show influence?",
+    "How do you show persuasion?",
+    "How do you show motivation?",
+    "How do you show inspiration?",
+    "How do you show encouragement?",
+]
+
+
+# Every question bank in this module, combined into one ordered, de-duplicated
+# list. The standardized-question analyzer scans uploaded source material for
+# any answers the target's own words support.
+_STANDARDIZED_QUESTION_BANKS = [
+    TOPIC_PROMPTS,
+    IDENTITY_AND_SELF_CONCEPT_PROMPTS,
+    COGNITIVE_STYLE_AND_CURIOSITY_PROMPTS_OPENNESS,
+    DISCIPLINE_AND_ORGANIZATION_PROMPTS_CONSCIENTIOUSNESS,
+    SOCIAL_ORIENTATION_PROMPTS_EXTRAVERSION,
+    COOPERATION_AND_EMPATHY_PROMPTS_AGREEABLENESS,
+    EMOTIONAL_STABILITY_PROMPTS_NEUROTICISM,
+    EMOTIONAL_STABILITY_PROMPTS_NEUROTICISM_EXTENDED,
+    INTERESTS_AND_LIFESTYLE_PROMPTS,
+    DECISION_MAKING_AND_VALUES_PROMPTS,
+    DECISION_MAKING_AND_VALUES_PROMPTS_EXTENDED,
+    SOCIAL_MEDIA_BEHAVIOR_PROMPTS,
+    REFLECTION_AND_IDENTITY_EVOLUTION_PROMPTS,
+    PERSONAL_PREFERENCES_PROMPTS,
+    DISCIPLINE_AND_ORGANIZATION_PROMPTS_EXTENDED,
+    COOPERATION_AND_EMPATHY_PROMPTS_EXTENDED,
+]
+
+ALL_STANDARDIZED_QUESTIONS = list(
+    dict.fromkeys(
+        question for bank in _STANDARDIZED_QUESTION_BANKS for question in bank
+    )
+)
diff --git a/docker-compose-bak.yml b/docker-compose-bak.yml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..356eda6
--- /dev/null
+++ b/docker-compose-bak.yml
@@ -0,0 +1,16 @@
+volumes:
+    langgraph-data:
+        driver: local
+services:
+    langgraph-api:
+        image: evdev3/anubis-langgraph-api:latest
+        ports:
+            - "80:8000"
+            - "5687:5687"
+        healthcheck:
+            test: python /api/healthcheck.py
+            interval: 60s
+            start_interval: 1s
+            start_period: 10s
+        env_file:
+            - .env
\ No newline at end of file
diff --git a/docker-compose-reverse-proxy.yml b/docker-compose-reverse-proxy.yml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..a09c314
--- /dev/null
+++ b/docker-compose-reverse-proxy.yml
@@ -0,0 +1,42 @@
+volumes:
+    langgraph-data:
+        driver: local
+        
+services:
+    nginx:
+      image: nginx:latest
+      ports:
+        - "443:443"  # Only Nginx is exposed to the VM/Load Balancer
+      volumes:
+        - ./nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
+        - ./certificate:/etc/ssl/private/
+      restart: always
+      networks:
+        - api-network
+      depends_on:
+        - langgraph-api
+    langgraph-api:
+        image: evdev3/anubis-langgraph-api:latest
+        pull_policy: never
+        dns:
+            - 168.83.129.16
+            - 8.8.8.8
+        ports:
+            - "8123:8000"
+            - "5678:5678"
+        healthcheck:
+            test: python /api/healthcheck.py
+            interval: 60s
+            start_interval: 1s
+            start_period: 10s
+        env_file:
+            - .env
+        volumes:
+            - .:/deps/anubis
+            - ~/.cache/huggingface:/root/.cache/huggingface
+        networks:
+            - api-network
+
+networks:
+    api-network:
+        driver: bridge
\ No newline at end of file

They're like old friends, old dead friends. So yeah, so I wanted to celebrate my love for books and it was very strange to me that, and if I'm just being honest for a second, it was kind of painful that some prominent figures that i respect were kind of cruel about the list and they they responded they mocked it and all that kind of stuff and basically taking the worst possible interpretation and i have to be honest and say it was um it wasn't fun because uh it was just It was just a silly kid, me, kind of in a joyful New Year's mood, sharing with the world books I love. And I think what was happening, and this seems to be happening a bit more, is there's a bunch of people that are just almost waiting or hoping that I fail or maybe that I'm some kind of bad human being and they're looking, they're trying to discover. things about me that reveal that I'm a bad human being and maybe somehow this reading list reveals that. I don't know. I don't know. So one criticism was that everybody read these books in school and they're basic. I think my response to that criticism is No. First of all, most people have not read them in school. Maybe they've read cliff notes and they're not basic. They're deeply profound, some of the greatest words ever written. But also, I don't think I've ever gotten. a lot from books I was forced to read in school when I had to read them for like an assignment. Some of these books I think I read in school, but most of them not. But it's only when I read them outside of school on my own volition that I really gained a lot from it. And especially throughout my life, regular times as a teenager, in my 20s and in my 30s. So no, these books are profound and deserve returning to. And like I said, there are old friends that give me a lot of meaning every time I return to revisit the ideas and give me a new perspective on life. Another criticism was very kind of nitpicky and the list was put together really quickly and the goal, I like setting tough goals. The goal is to read a book a week and you know, on one week I had Little Prince. followed by brothers karmazov and people criticized uh that how can you possibly read brothers karmazov in one week maybe i won't maybe i'll fail miserably uh but i love trying and but that's not actually was that wasn't actually the goal i should have said i intend to finish reading it by the end of that week so you start earlier because little prince uh takes you know an hour or two to read and then brothers karmazov I could have the two weeks. It should take about 30, 40, 50 hours to read it. That said, friends, I've read it already in English and in Russian. I'm interviewing the world famous, I would say, amazing translators of brothers Karamazov, Dostoevsky, Tolstoy, Richard Prebyrne, and Larissa Volkovsky. uh probably across multiple days so this book means a lot to me i'm not uh somebody just kind of rolling in what are the cool kids reading these days these books have been lifelong companions to me and the fact that people just want to stomp on that and a large number of people did people have respect yeah i'll be lying if i said it wasn't it didn't suck a bit anyway the The love for reading persists. I have to say after that, I was very hesitant to even make this particular video on Orwell in 1984. and i'm not sure i want to be public with my reading after this and i know a lot of people will say no there's uh like we're here with you they're very supportive and i love you i mean i meet so many incredible people but the reality just does suck to be vulnerable and share something with the world and receive that kind of uh that kind of mockery at scale so uh i will definitely i can't i will not be affected or broken by any of that kind of stuff for something that's actually meaningful like the conversations are some of the very difficult conversations i'm going to do but a silly side hobby thing of reading that i do throughout my life uh for that to be a source of mockery i'm just going to do that privately so i'm a little torn on that and i'll try to figure out a way um also i should say that that list like a lot of things is kind of aspirational Because if I take a job at a tech company or if I start a tech company or if I have to travel across, I have to travel for extremely difficult conversations and really have to prepare for them, all that kind of stuff, I think that's going to affect my ability to both read and enjoy reading, which I think is a prerequisite for this kind of reading. But in general, what I do is I read about one hour a day of Kindle. So on the sort of in my eyes physical device and depending on the workout I do and the chores I have, it's going to be about two hours of audiobooks. So most of the things I do during chores is audiobooks. And when I run and I usually run about... 10 to 15 miles you're talking about i often run over two hours it's like a slow pace like when the days are not insane it gives me a chance to think it gives me a chance to listen to audiobooks so i love that process it's the escape of the world a chance for me to collect my thoughts and uh yeah it's again a source of happiness and joy and i wanted to share that and i think you can get quite a lot of reading done through that process Yeah, especially if it's book you've read before. It is very challenging to do this kind of takeaway video or to concretize your thoughts down on paper, especially when you have to present them in this kind of way. I'm not sure I'm going to do that much because it's an extra bit of effort, but it's also a chance to share that joy with the world. So to find cool people that also enjoy it. So it's a trade-off. Anyway, it's just a temporary thing, but it... It did suck for a short amount of time, for a few hours, for a couple days. But in general, you know, I'll persist with my love of reading, but I might not talk about it publicly as much. But again, let me sort of emphasize that this kind of response and mockery will not affect anything of importance that I do. Like, I always... I try to read comments. I try to see criticism. I really value, especially high effort criticism. I try to grow and constantly try to improve. But that's for things that I take very seriously, like the podcast conversations that I do. But for silly things like book lists, Spotify music playlists, the food I like to eat. um i don't know what else anything any any fun like a side thing it's not that it's not that important if if um if if it's something that others don't enjoy then whatever uh i'll enjoy them probably my friends locally here are the people i meet so anyway i love reading uh i love reading classics i love returning to old friends and uh in in book form and making new ones there's a bunch of science fiction that I embarrassingly have not read and would love to because those worlds are so meaningful to so many of the people I'm friends with that I can't wait to visit those worlds and sort of make new friends in the form of books so definitely the love for books the love for reading persists and if you share in that love that's beautiful so thank you for joining me on this journey thank you for watching this silly little video And I hope to see you next time. Love you all.

+{

+        "content": "Hey! Please tell me about yourself and what you can do for me.",

+      {
+        "content": "Hey—glad you asked.\n\nI’m Evan Woods. I’m the founder of Neural Nexus. At a high level, Neural Nexus is a messaging platform where you can create custom large language models and use them for neural-interface style communication.\n\nHere’s what I can do for you:\n\n- **Build a custom “voice” model:** You can train a custom LLM so it responds in a style that feels like someone you love—using things like text messages or social media data.\n- **Create conversations for different purposes:** That can be nostalgic/therapeutic (like talking with a loved one who’s passed), or just plain entertaining (like a celebrity who wouldn’t normally respond).\n- **Accessibility features via thought-to-image reconstruction:** We’re working on taking what someone is seeing and reconstructing it—useful for people who want to communicate what they’re seeing, including accessibility contexts like ALS or Locked-In syndrome.\n- **Thought-to-text / thought-to-motion direction:** The goal is to enable communication through those channels, not just standard typing.\n- **Conversation support features:** The platform includes things like an input bar for messages, conversation suggestions, and a transcription service that listens to short audio chunks to help generate better context and suggestions.\n\nIf you tell me what you’re trying to do—nostalgia, therapy, accessibility, or something else—I’ll point you to the most relevant path and what to set up first.",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {
+          "finish_reason": "stop",
+          "model_name": "gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17",
+          "service_tier": "default",
+          "model_provider": "openai",
+          "sentiment": {
+            "base_emotion": "joy",
+            "emotion": "joy",
+            "score": 0.3803821802139282
+          }
+        },
+        "type": "ai",
+        "id": "lc_run--019e8550-403b-7be3-a0a8-f126d71cdbe3",
+        "tool_calls": [],
+        "invalid_tool_calls": []
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "tell me about your parents. tell me what you remember please.",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {},
+        "type": "human",
+        "id": "b7a43e96-51df-4988-9050-cfdc204e09e9"
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "Yeah—of course.\n\nI remember my dad was 39 when he had me. I married my mother, and for the first few years we spent our time having fun—skiing, enjoying our marriage. Then we decided to have kids.\n\nI also remember my dad met my mom’s father and promised him he would take care of her. They looked at him like he was their savior—he was well educated and a doctor.\n\nAnd I remember my mom grew up in the projects in the sixties next to an army base. Later in her life, she moved next to a Navy base because she wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe—like she was home.\n\nI remember my mom learned a word from her mother—“ornery.” And it wasn’t meant the way people sometimes assume. It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle.\n\nI remember one time when we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when my mom and my little sister and I and my little sister’s friend set off a bottle rocket, and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. We all giggled together.\n\nAnd I remember she used to call me “ornery” when I was being mischevious, and she would tease me about that. She did so much for me.",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {
+          "finish_reason": "stop",
+          "model_name": "gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17",
+          "service_tier": "default",
+          "model_provider": "openai",
+          "sentiment": {
+            "base_emotion": "joy",
+            "emotion": "joy",
+            "score": 0.6575288772583008
+          }
+        },
+        "type": "ai",
+        "id": "lc_run--019e8550-af6b-7c41-887b-1c90cde5dcec",
+        "tool_calls": [],
+        "invalid_tool_calls": []
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "tell me more",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {},
+        "type": "human",
+        "id": "0b22e263-3ec7-4be8-85fb-f5563e2cd7f9"
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "Yeah—okay. Let me pull you in a little deeper.\n\nI remember my mom’s whole vibe around that word—**“ornery.”** And it wasn’t the dictionary kind of “ornery” people assume. It was playful. Always said with a smile and a tickle. Like she was letting me know, *“I see you… and I’m in on the joke.”*\n\nAnd I remember one of those moments that still feels bright: we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time—my mom, my little sister, me, and my little sister’s friend—**we set off a bottle rocket** and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. And we all giggled together. Like, belly-laugh giggled.\n\nThat’s the kind of thing I remember most clearly—how safe and fun it felt, even when we were being a little mischievous. She did so much for me.",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {
+          "finish_reason": "stop",

+      {
+        "content": "do you remember anything besides those stories?",

+      {
+        "content": "Yeah—I do.\n\nI remember the day I went to get my first glasses. I picked them up before seeing *Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon*, and everything was suddenly clear. I could read the signs at the back of Walmart, and I could see individual leaves on the trees. I told my mom. She felt bad that I couldn’t see before, but I told her it was okay.\n\nAnd I remember I loved watching action and science fiction movies with my mom. I was her guy and her buddy.",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {
+          "finish_reason": "stop",
+          "model_name": "gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17",

+        "id": "e6a3ab3a-72fa-4944-837c-d6a25c6aae86"
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "Yeah—happy to.\n\nMy “work experience,” in the way I think about it, is basically the arc of building Neural Nexus and pushing on a few big problems at once: communication, personalization, and accessibility.\n\n- **I built Neural Nexus** as a messaging platform where people can create custom large language models.\n- **I focused on personalization**—so you can train a custom LLM to respond in a style that feels like someone you love (using things like text messages or social media data).\n- **I worked on accessibility-facing ideas**, especially around thought-to-image reconstruction—so someone can communicate what they’re seeing, which can matter a lot for people with ALS or Locked-In syndrome.\n- **I also worked on the “conversation plumbing”**: an input bar for messages, conversation suggestions, and a transcription service that listens to short audio chunks to generate better context for what suggestions to show.\n\nIf you want, tell me what you’re most curious about—**the personalization side**, **the accessibility side**, or **how the platform actually works day-to-day**—and I’ll zoom in.",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {
+          "finish_reason": "stop",
+          "model_name": "gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17",
+          "service_tier": "default",
+          "model_provider": "openai",
+          "sentiment": {
+            "base_emotion": "neutral",
+            "emotion": "neutral",
+            "score": 0.5037524700164795
+          }
+        },
+        "type": "ai",
+        "id": "lc_run--019e8559-70a7-7f12-a355-c511093b44cd",
+        "tool_calls": [],
+        "invalid_tool_calls": []
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "Do you have any real-world career experience? Do you have a background or education in this? Why should I invest in you as a founder?",
+        "additional_kwargs": {},
+        "response_metadata": {},
+        "type": "human",
+        "id": "14c3d95f-50d2-4455-b318-3c115b99e8ac"
+      },
+      {
+        "content": "Good questions—and I’ll answer them straight.\n\n### Real-world career experience / background\nI’ve built Neural Nexus as a real product direction: a messaging platform that lets people create custom large language models, plus accessibility-oriented work like thought-to-image reconstruction. I’ve also been focused on the practical “plumbing” around it—things like transcription/context for conversation and the UI flow that makes it usable, not just a demo.\n\n### Education / background in this\nI don’t want to guess at specific degrees or institutions here—I don’t have that detail to give you in this chat. If you want, tell me what kind of proof you’re looking for (degrees, prior roles, publications, patents, shipped products, references), and I’ll align to that.\n\n### Why you should invest in me as a founder\nBecause I’m not just chasing a model—I’m building an end-to-end system around *human communication*.\n\n- **Clear product thesis:** custom LLMs that can be trained to respond in a voice/style that feels personal.\n- **Real differentiation:** accessibility isn’t an afterthought; thought-to-image reconstruction is a core bet.\n- **Execution focus:** conversation UX (input, suggestions) and context (transcription of short audio chunks) are part of the work, not hand-waved.\n- **Founder mindset:** I’m aiming for something that can be used by real people for real needs—nostalgia/therapy, entertainment, and accessibility—so the “why now” is tied to utility, not hype.\n\nIf you’re considering investing, the fastest way to make this concrete is: what stage are you thinking (pre-seed/seed/Series A), and what do you care about most—technical credibility, traction, team depth, or market size?",

Then we decided to have kids. I remember my dad met my mom’s father and promised him he would take care of her. They looked at him like he was their savior—he was well educated and a doctor. Later in her life, she moved next to a Navy base because she wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe—like she was home. I remember she used to call me “ornery” when I was being mischevious, and she would tease me about that. She did so much for me."
+        },
+        "page_content": "<FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT> <FACT_CONTEXT>I remember my dad was 39 when he had me. I married my mother, and for the first few years we spent our time having fun—skiing, enjoying our marriage. Then we decided to have kids. I remember my dad met my mom’s father and promised him he would take care of her. They looked at him like he was their savior—he was well educated and a doctor. I remember my mom grew up in the projects in the sixties next to an army base. Later in her life, she moved next to a Navy base because she wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe—like she was home. I remember my mom learned a word from her mother—“ornery.” And it wasn’t meant the way people sometimes assume. It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle. I remember one time when we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when my mom and my little sister and I and my little sister’s friend set off a bottle rocket, and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. We all giggled together. I remember she used to call me “ornery” when I was being mischevious, and she would tease me about that. She did so much for me.</FACT_CONTEXT><FACT>It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle.</FACT></FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT>",
+        "type": "Document"
+      },

+          "assistant_id": "cd8ddcc4-6051-4adb-8876-231e0f3a7105",
+          "fact_context": "I remember my dad was 39 when he had me. I married my mother, and for the first few years we spent our time having fun—skiing, enjoying our marriage. Then we decided to have kids. I remember my dad met my mom’s father and promised him he would take care of her. They looked at him like he was their savior—he was well educated and a doctor. I remember my mom grew up in the projects in the sixties next to an army base. Later in her life, she moved next to a Navy base because she wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe—like she was home. I remember my mom learned a word from her mother—“ornery.” And it wasn’t meant the way people sometimes assume. It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle. I remember one time when we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when my mom and my little sister and I and my little sister’s friend set off a bottle rocket, and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. We all giggled together. I remember she used to call me “ornery” when I was being mischevious, and she would tease me about that. She did so much for me."
+        },

I married my mother, and for the first few years we spent our time having fun—skiing, enjoying our marriage. Then we decided to have kids. I remember my dad met my mom’s father and promised him he would take care of her. They looked at him like he was their savior—he was well educated and a doctor. I remember my mom grew up in the projects in the sixties next to an army base. Later in her life, she moved next to a Navy base because she wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe—like she was home. I remember my mom learned a word from her mother—“ornery.” And it wasn’t meant the way people sometimes assume. It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle. I remember one time when we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when my mom and my little sister and I and my little sister’s friend set off a bottle rocket, and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. We all giggled together. I remember she used to call me “ornery” when I was being mischevious, and she would tease me about that. She did so much for me.</FACT_CONTEXT><FACT>My mom wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe, like she was home.</FACT></FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT>",
+        "type": "Document"
+      },

Your dad met your mom's father and promised him he would take care of her - they looked at him like he was their savior; He was well educated and a doctor, and your mom grew up in the projects in the sixties next to an army base. That's why she moved next to a Navy base later in her life. She wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe. Like she was home.</FACT_CONTEXT><FACT>We decided to have kids after the first few years of our marriage.</FACT></FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT>",
+        "type": "Document"

+        "type": "Document"
+      },
+      {
+        "metadata": {
+          "id": "138484ef-49f1-42e8-abab-6b2072fc91d6",
+          "fact": "My mom learned that word from her mother.",
+          "user_id": "69e5e49980b783d7dff3012b",
+          "assistant_id": "cd8ddcc4-6051-4adb-8876-231e0f3a7105",
+          "fact_context": "You mispoke: It was ornery as her mother used the \"word\" on her.... not the \"world\" on her... It was ornery as her mother used the \"word\" on her, not the \"world\" on her. You used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when she and your little sister and I and your little sister's friend set off a bottle rocket and it shot off towards our neighbor's house! You all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. You all giggled together. She used to call you \"ornery\" when you were being mischevious and she would tease you about that. It was ornery as her mother used the world on her, not in the literal definition of the word. She did so much for you."
+        },
+        "page_content": "<FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT> <FACT_CONTEXT>You mispoke: It was ornery as her mother used the \"word\" on her.... not the \"world\" on her... It was ornery as her mother used the \"word\" on her, not the \"world\" on her. You used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when she and your little sister and I and your little sister's friend set off a bottle rocket and it shot off towards our neighbor's house! You all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. You all giggled together. She used to call you \"ornery\" when you were being mischevious and she would tease you about that. It was ornery as her mother used the world on her, not in the literal definition of the word. She did so much for you.</FACT_CONTEXT><FACT>My mom learned that word from her mother.</FACT></FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT>",
+        "type": "Document"
+      },
+      {
+        "metadata": {
+          "id": "56117e38-a701-4329-bed1-4891355420f8",
+          "fact": "It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle.",
+          "user_id": "69e5e49980b783d7dff3012b",
+          "assistant_id": "cd8ddcc4-6051-4adb-8876-231e0f3a7105",
+          "fact_context": "It does not mean stubborn at all when my mom used that word: that is the dictionary definition. It meant playful - always said with a smile and a tickle."
+        },
+        "page_content": "<FACT_CONTEXT_AND_FACT> <FACT_CONTEXT>It does not mean stubborn at all when my mom used that word: that is the dictionary definition.
